1221	\subsection{The obstruction, the open core, and the conjecture}
1222	
1223	\begin{proposition}[No-anchor obstruction]\label{prop:noanchor}
1224	Every pair $(S,T)$ of $s$-subsets of $D$ is simultaneously an agreement pattern of some received word ($U$ with interpolant $L_{S\cup T}\cdot G$ has both explaining codewords zero).  Hence no word-free pair condition exists, lines with arbitrary $f,g$ admit no characteristic-zero anchor, and methods that certify statements by averaging over primes --- bounding, for each characteristic-zero object, the number of primes at which it degenerates, as in every density theorem of this paper --- are structurally incapable of bounding $\emca$ over \emph{all} lines, the words being quantified after the prime.  The all-lines positive half rests entirely on fixed-prime technology.
1225	\end{proposition}
1226	
1227	\begin{problem}[Per-fiber collision problem]\label{prob:perfiber}
1228	For $\sigma\ge Cn/\log_2n$ and all large $p\equiv1\pmod n$ with $n^{c_0}\le p\le2^{o(n)}$: every fiber of the prefix map $\Phi_\sigma(A)=(e_1(A),\dots,e_\sigma(A))$ on $s$-subsets of $H$ contains at most $n^{O(1)}$ ordered pairs that are prefix-equal mod $\mathfrak p$ but not in $\Z[\zeta]$.  This single divisibility statement implies the monomial-line positive half throughout the window, beyond the density and stable regimes proved above; the natural attacks --- prime averaging (blocked at fixed $p$ by construction), the polynomial method (whose Nullstellensatz coefficients on subgroup grids become complete character sums), subgroup exponential sums (whose known ranges end at $N\ge p^{3/7+\eps}$), and anticoncentration technology (whose forward inequalities reduce to the same even-moment counts) --- all terminate at it.
1229	\end{problem}

1234	        \eta_n\ge(1+\eps)\taustar(\rho,q_{D,n})
1235	\]
1236	for some fixed $\eps>0$, and
1237	\[
1238	        \mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)\le B_Q\log_2 n+O(\log\log n)

1241	\[
1242	        \max_t\Lambda^{\rm aper}_{t,\,1-\rho-\eta_n}(H_n,k_n)\le n^{1+o(1)},
1243	\]
1244	and, after the tangent-floor and generated-field corrections, the raw error obeys the two-term bound

1248	        \frac{n^{1+o(1)}+2^{(\beta(\rho)/\HH(\rho))\mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)(1+o(1))}}{q_{D,n}}

The quotient term is not removable: \cref{thm:qnecessity} gives the exact monomial-line lower bound $\Acl(N^*,\rho N^*+1)/q$ above the quotient norm threshold, and the profile-asymptotic form $2^{(\beta/\HH)\mathcal Q_H(\eta)(1-o(1))}/q$ when $N^*\to\infty$. 1255	\begin{remark}[Aperiodic packing normalization]\label{rem:aper}

1259	\section{The quotient-exact floor, and the necessity of the quotient profile}\label{sec:qfloor}
1260	
1261	The descent floor of \cref{thm:diag}(i) counts canonical-line bad slopes produced on a quotient.  At quotient order $N'=n/\sigma$ and quotient agreement size $\ell'=\rho N'+1$, the Dias da Silva--Hamidoune lower bound gives only the no-threshold floor $\rho(1-\rho)(N')^2+O(N')$.  The closed-form quotient count $\Acl(N',\ell')=2^{\beta(\rho)N'(1-o(1))}$ from \cref{thm:exactcount} is exponentially larger in $N'$, but the proof of exactness there used the full-domain stable range $p>(2s)^{n/2}$.  The point of this section is that, for the floor alone, only quotient-level collisions matter.  The relevant norm bound is therefore $(2\ell')^{N'/2}$, which is quasipolynomial at safe slack, not exponential in $n\log n$.

1263	Throughout this section
1264	\[
1265	        \beta(\rho)=\frac12\max_{0\le\theta\le 2\min(\rho,1-\rho)}\bigl(\HH(\theta)+\theta\bigr),
1266	        \qquad
1267	        \Acl(N',\rho N'+1)=2^{\beta(\rho)N'(1-o(1))},
1268	\]
1269	as in \cref{thm:exactcount}. 1275	\[
1276	        p>(2\ell')^{N'/2},
1277	\]
1278	then the canonical line $x^{k+\sigma}+zx^k$ at radius $1-\rho-1/N'$ has at least
1279	\[

\begin{proof}
By \cref{thm:stable}(i), $B(p)$ is the number of distinct values of $e_1(B)$ for $B\in\binom{\mu_{N'}}{\ell'}$.  Two such subsets have equal $e_1$ in $\Z[\zeta_{N'}]$ iff their difference $h$ satisfies $\widehat h(1)=0$, which by \cref{thm:rigidcyclo} is exactly antipodal-pair rearrangement.  Choose $t$ singleton antipodal pairs and their signs, and choose $u$ full pairs; feasibility is $t+2u=\ell'$ and $u\le n_1-t$, giving the displayed sum.  The stable range prevents distinct characteristic-zero values from colliding modulo $\mathfrak p$.  At $\rho=1/2$ the parity-restricted trinomial sum equals $\frac12(3^{n_1}-1)$.  The asymptotic formula is the entropy maximization of $\binom{n_1}{t}2^t$ under the feasibility cap $t\le\min(\ell',N'-\ell')$ built into the sum: the constraint $t\le\ell'$ binds for $\rho\le1/2$ and $t\le N'-\ell'$, from $u\le n_1-t$, binds for $\rho>1/2$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Where the stable threshold sits]\label{rem:threshold}
$(2s)^{n/2}$ lies above the density windows of \cref{thm:diag,thm:allbases}.  Those theorems can be viewed as averaged-prime descents of the exact regime.  Empirically, collapse may occur far earlier, but the stable threshold is the price of every-prime certainty.
\end{remark}

\section{The boundary, and the conjecture}\label{sec:boundary}

\subsection{The exact normal form}

The positive theorems above concern monomial lines.  The general question is posed exactly by the following coordinates.  Throughout, $s_\delta=\lceil(1-\delta)n\rceil$ and $r=n-k$.

\begin{definition}[Residue-line datum and packing number]\label{def:residue}
Fix $1\le t\le r$.  A \emph{degree-$t$ residue-line datum} over $(\F,D,k)$ is a denominator $E\in\F[X]$ of degree $t$ nonzero on $D$, a numerator $B$ with $\deg B<t$, and an anchor word $w:D\to\F$.  A \emph{witness} for slope $z$ at radius $\delta$ is $(Q_z,S_z)$ with $\deg Q_z<k+t$, $|S_z|\ge s_\delta$, $Q_z\equiv zB\pmod E$, and $Q_z=w$ on $S_z$; it is \emph{noncontained} if no $A,G\in\F_{<k}[X]$ satisfy $A=w/E$ and $G=-B/E$ on $S_z$.  Let $\Lambda^{\rm NC}_{t,\delta}(D,k)$ be the maximum, over data, of the number of slopes admitting a noncontained witness.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Denominator representation]\label{lem:denom}
For a word $b:D\to\F$ and $E$ of degree $t$ nonzero on $D$: a representation $b=R-B/E$ on $D$ with $\deg R<k$, $\deg B<t$ exists iff the word $Eb$ extends to a polynomial of degree $<k+t$ on $D$; for $t=r$ this always holds, by interpolation.  (Euclidean division of the extension by $E$, both ways.)
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Exact normal form]\label{thm:normalform}
If some polynomial of degree $r$ is nonzero on $D$ (automatic for multiplicative domains, via $X^r$), then for every $\delta$:
\[
        \emca(C,\delta)\ =\ \frac1q\,\max_{1\le t\le r}\,\Lambda^{\rm NC}_{t,\delta}(D,k) .
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
($\le$)  Fix a line $u_z=f+zg$ with bad-slope set $\Gamma$ and witnesses $u_z=P_z$ on $S_z$.  By \cref{lem:denom} at $t=r$ write $g=R-B/E$ on $D$; set $w=E\cdot f$ and $Q_z=E(P_z-zR)+zB$.  Then $\deg Q_z<k+t$, $Q_z\equiv zB\pmod E$, and on $S_z$: $Q_z=E(f+zg-zR)+zB=Ef-zB+zB=w$.  Containment would yield degree-$<k$ explanations of both $f$ and $g$ on $S_z$, contradicting badness (ii); all of $\Gamma$ uses one datum.
($\ge$)  Given a datum and noncontained-witnessed slopes, the line $f=w/E$, $g=-B/E$ has, via $P_z=(Q_z-zB)/E$ (degree $<k$ by count), $P_z=f+zg$ on $S_z$: condition (i); noncontainedness is verbatim condition (ii).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Strata]\label{rem:strata}
The monomial slack line $x^{k+T}+zx^k$ on a subgroup is the datum $(E,B,w)=(X^r,-1,x^T)$ --- maximal denominator, simplest numerator --- and its noncontained slope set is exactly $\mathcal B_T(D,k)$ (\cref{thm:exactslack}): every theorem of \cref{sec:positive,sec:directions,sec:stable} is a statement about this stratum and its quotient refinements.  \Cref{thm:onez} is the trivial packing bound ($\Lambda^{\rm NC}\le2^n$), and the tangent floor (\cref{prop:floor}) is a residue-line family with $\Lambda^{\rm NC}\ge\lfloor\delta n\rfloor$, which is why \cref{conj:B} below carries the $n^{1+o(1)}/q$ correction --- the floor saturates it, so the conjectured positive half is sharp if true.
\end{remark}

\begin{proposition}[Denominator closure as a coordinate normal form]\label{thm:closure}
In the coordinate formulation of line obstructions, assuming $|S_z|>k$ for active rows, every obstruction class is either zero, tangent (the direction word is explained on the active coordinates but the anchor is not), or a residue-line datum as in \cref{def:residue}.  Conversely, every residue-line datum gives such a class.  This proposition is a normal-form statement only; it does not bound the packing number $\Lambda^{\rm NC}$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
If the direction word $b$ is explained by a degree-$<k$ polynomial on the active set, subtract that global completion; the remaining class is zero or tangent according as the anchor is explained.  Otherwise \cref{lem:denom} writes $b=R-B/E$.  Subtracting the global direction $R$ reduces to $b=-B/E$ with $B\ne0$, and multiplying the row relation by $E$ gives $EP_z+zB=w$ on $S_z$, exactly the residue-line form.  The converse is obtained by reversing these formulas.
\end{proof}

\subsection{The obstruction, the open core, and the conjecture}

\begin{proposition}[No-anchor obstruction]\label{prop:noanchor}
Every pair $(S,T)$ of $s$-subsets of $D$ is simultaneously an agreement pattern of some received word ($U$ with interpolant $L_{S\cup T}\cdot G$ has both explaining codewords zero).  Hence no word-free pair condition exists, lines with arbitrary $f,g$ admit no characteristic-zero anchor, and methods that certify statements by averaging over primes --- bounding, for each characteristic-zero object, the number of primes at which it degenerates, as in every density theorem of this paper --- are structurally incapable of bounding $\emca$ over \emph{all} lines, the words being quantified after the prime.  The all-lines positive half rests entirely on fixed-prime technology.
\end{proposition}

\begin{problem}[Per-fiber collision problem]\label{prob:perfiber}
For $\sigma\ge Cn/\log_2n$ and all large $p\equiv1\pmod n$ with $n^{c_0}\le p\le2^{o(n)}$: every fiber of the prefix map $\Phi_\sigma(A)=(e_1(A),\dots,e_\sigma(A))$ on $s$-subsets of $H$ contains at most $n^{O(1)}$ ordered pairs that are prefix-equal mod $\mathfrak p$ but not in $\Z[\zeta]$.  This single divisibility statement implies the monomial-line positive half throughout the window, beyond the density and stable regimes proved above; the natural attacks --- prime averaging (blocked at fixed $p$ by construction), the polynomial method (whose Nullstellensatz coefficients on subgroup grids become complete character sums), subgroup exponential sums (whose known ranges end at $N\ge p^{3/7+\eps}$), and anticoncentration technology (whose forward inequalities reduce to the same even-moment counts) --- all terminate at it.
\end{problem}

\begin{conjecture}[Slack MCA threshold, floor- and quotient-corrected]\label{conj:B}
Let $H_n\le\F_{q_n}^\times$ be smooth of order $n$, $k_n=\rho n$, $\mathrm{poly}(n)\le q_n\le2^{o(n)}$, and let $q_{D,n}$ be the generated-field size.  Suppose $\eta_n$ clears the corrected list-side scales,
\[
        \eta_n\ge(1+\eps)\taustar(\rho,q_{D,n})
\]
for some fixed $\eps>0$, and
\[
        \mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)\le B_Q\log_2 n+O(\log\log n)
\]
for some fixed profile exponent $B_Q$.  With $\Lambda^{\rm aper}$ the quotient-separated packing number of \cref{rem:aper}, the positive MCA prediction is
\[
        \max_t\Lambda^{\rm aper}_{t,\,1-\rho-\eta_n}(H_n,k_n)\le n^{1+o(1)},
\]
and, after the tangent-floor and generated-field corrections, the raw error obeys the two-term bound
\[
        \emca(C_n,1-\rho-\eta_n)
        \le
        \frac{n^{1+o(1)}+2^{(\beta(\rho)/\HH(\rho))\mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)(1+o(1))}}{q_{D,n}}
        \le
        \frac{n^{1+o(1)}+n^{(\beta(\rho)/\HH(\rho))B_Q+o(1)}}{q_{D,n}}.
\]
In particular $\emca(C_n,1-\rho-\eta_n)\le n^{1+o(1)}/q_{D,n}$ whenever the profile obeys the stronger bound $\mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)\le(\HH(\rho)/\beta(\rho))\log_2n+o(\log n)$.  The quotient term is not removable: \cref{thm:qnecessity} gives the exact monomial-line lower bound $\Acl(N^*,\rho N^*+1)/q$ above the quotient norm threshold, and the profile-asymptotic form $2^{(\beta/\HH)\mathcal Q_H(\eta)(1-o(1))}/q$ when $N^*\to\infty$.  Thus, in that asymptotic quotient range, a target raw bound $n^A/q$ requires $B_Q\le(\HH(\rho)/\beta(\rho))A$, equivalently $\mathcal Q_H(\eta)\lesssim(\HH(\rho)/\beta(\rho))A\log_2n$.  The failure half remains a separate conditional prediction: below the generated-field entropy gap, MCA error should be $1-o(1)$ only in strata where the relevant power-sum or residue-line map is sufficiently equidistributed to hit almost every slope.  Coefficient pigeonhole and quotient-core mechanisms prove list-side obstructions and motivate this failure prediction, but they do not by themselves force many bad slopes of an arbitrary line.
\end{conjecture}

\begin{remark}[Aperiodic packing normalization]\label{rem:aper}
The phrase ``after quotient-periodic denominator families are separated'' in \cref{conj:B} and \cref{conj:final-mca} can be made precise.  Call a residue line in the normal form of \cref{thm:normalform} \emph{quotient-periodic} if its denominator is a pullback through a proper quotient $x\mapsto x^M$ with $M\mid\gcd(n,k)$, $M>1$, and let $\Lambda^{\rm aper}_{t,\delta}(H,k)$ be the maximum size of a noncontained packing containing no quotient-periodic line.  The conjectures assert $\max_t\Lambda^{\rm aper}_{t,1-\rho-\eta_n}(H_n,k_n)\le n^{1+o(1)}$, while the removed quotient-periodic lines are predicted to contribute at most the displayed quotient-profile term, matching the lower bound of \cref{thm:qnecessity}.
\end{remark}

\section{The quotient-exact floor, and the necessity of the quotient profile}\label{sec:qfloor}

The descent floor of \cref{thm:diag}(i) counts canonical-line bad slopes produced on a quotient.  At quotient order $N'=n/\sigma$ and quotient agreement size $\ell'=\rho N'+1$, the Dias da Silva--Hamidoune lower bound gives only the no-threshold floor $\rho(1-\rho)(N')^2+O(N')$.  The closed-form quotient count $\Acl(N',\ell')=2^{\beta(\rho)N'(1-o(1))}$ from \cref{thm:exactcount} is exponentially larger in $N'$, but the proof of exactness there used the full-domain stable range $p>(2s)^{n/2}$.  The point of this section is that, for the floor alone, only quotient-level collisions matter.  The relevant norm bound is therefore $(2\ell')^{N'/2}$, which is quasipolynomial at safe slack, not exponential in $n\log n$.

Throughout this section
\[
        \beta(\rho)=\frac12\max_{0\le\theta\le 2\min(\rho,1-\rho)}\bigl(\HH(\theta)+\theta\bigr),
        \qquad
        \Acl(N',\rho N'+1)=2^{\beta(\rho)N'(1-o(1))},
\]
as in \cref{thm:exactcount}.  Thus $\beta(\rho)=\frac12(\HH(2\rho)+2\rho)$ for $\rho<1/3$, $\beta(\rho)=\frac12\log_23$ for $1/3\le\rho\le2/3$, and $\beta(\rho)=\frac12(\HH(2(1-\rho))+2(1-\rho))$ for $\rho>2/3$; at the deployed rates $\rho\le1/2$ this agrees with the one-sided maximization over $\theta\le2\rho$.

\subsection{The quotient-exact floor}

\begin{proposition}[Quotient-exact floor]\label{prop:qfloor}
Let $D\le\F_p^\times$ be smooth of order $n$, let $C=\RS[\F_p,D,k]$ with $k=\rho n$, and let $N'\mid n$ satisfy $\rho N'\in\Z$ and $\ell'=\rho N'+1\le N'$.  Put $\sigma=n/N'$ and $Q=D^\sigma$, so $Q$ has order $N'$.  If
\[
        p>(2\ell')^{N'/2},
\]
then the canonical line $x^{k+\sigma}+zx^k$ at radius $1-\rho-1/N'$ has at least
\[
        \left|\{-e_1(B)\bmod\mathfrak p:\ B\in\tbinom{Q}{\ell'}\}\right|
        =\Acl(N',\ell')
\]
bad slopes, for every prime $p\equiv1\pmod n$ above the displayed threshold, where $\mathfrak p\subset\Z[\zeta_{N'}]$ is any degree-one prime above $p$; if $N'\to\infty$, then $\Acl(N',\ell')=2^{\beta(\rho)N'(1-o(1))}$ by \cref{thm:exactcount}.  Equivalently, above the quotient norm threshold, the descent part of the canonical bad-slope set is exactly the reduction of its characteristic-zero value set.  The threshold is
\[
        2^{(N'/2)\log_2(2\ell')},
\]
for example $2^{33.4}$ at $(N',\rho)=(16,1/2)$ and $2^{81.4}$ at $(32,1/2)$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By \cref{lem:tlocator} at $t=1$ and \cref{thm:slackt}, every value $z=-e_1(B)$ with $B\in\binom Q{\ell'}$ is support-wise bad for $x^{k+\sigma}+zx^k$ at radius $1-\rho-1/N'$.  It remains only to count the image of $e_1$ modulo $p$.

Fix a degree-one prime $\mathfrak p\subset\Z[\zeta_{N'}]$ over $p$, identifying $Q$ with $\mu_{N'}$.  If $B\ne B'$ have $e_1(B)\ne e_1(B')$ in $\Z[\zeta_{N'}]$, then $h=\mathbf 1_B-\mathbf 1_{B'}$ has $\widehat h(1)\ne0$.  Every archimedean conjugate of $\widehat h(1)$ has modulus at most $\|h\|_1\le2\ell'$, so
\[
        0<\left|\Norm_{\Q(\zeta_{N'})/\Q}\widehat h(1)\right|
        \le(2\ell')^{N'/2}<p.
\]
Thus $\mathfrak p\nmid\widehat h(1)$: distinct characteristic-zero values remain distinct modulo $\mathfrak p$.  By \cref{thm:rigidcyclo} and the class enumeration in \cref{thm:exactcount}, the number of distinct characteristic-zero values is exactly the antipodal-class count $\Acl(N',\ell')$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[The threshold is real]\label{rem:thresholdneeded}
Below the quotient norm threshold, quotient-level collisions can reduce the image below $\Acl(N',\ell')$.  At $(N',\ell')=(16,9)$ the image equals $3280=\Acl(16,9)$ for every tested prime $p\ge 67{,}057$, but drops to $3264$ at $p=58{,}337$ and $3232$ at $p=66{,}593$, and fluctuates in $[2576,3280]$ throughout $p\in[5281,66593]$.  Every observed deficit is a multiple of $16$, i.e. an $H$-orbit of collided classes, as forced by the dilation invariance of \cref{lem:dilation}; see V13 in \cref{app:verify}.  Thus an every-prime floor of the exact quotient size genuinely needs a norm threshold.  Below it, the appropriate unconditional statement is a density statement over primes, as in \cref{thm:diag}(ii).
\end{remark}

\begin{corollary}[Canonical line at safe slack, two-sided at every large quotient prime]\label{cor:diagupgrade}
In the setting of \cref{thm:diag}, put $N'=n/\sigma=(\log_2n)/\ceff$ and $\ell'=\rho N'+1$.  For every prime $p\equiv1\pmod n$ with
\[
        p>(2\ell')^{N'/2}=n^{O_{\ceff}(\log\log n)},
\]
one has
\[
        B(p)\ge \Acl(N',\ell')=n^{\beta(\rho)/\ceff\,(1-o(1))}.
\]
Moreover, for at least half the primes in the windows of \cref{thm:diag}(ii), the descent term is bounded by the same class count and hence
\[
        B(p)\le \Acl(N',\ell')+O_\rho(\log n).
\]
Where the two prime ranges overlap, the canonical-line count at safe slack is pinned at exponent $\beta(\rho)/\ceff$.
\end{corollary}

\subsection{Necessity of the quotient profile for MCA}

\begin{theorem}[The quotient profile is necessary for MCA at large primes]\label{thm:qnecessity}
Let $H_n\le\F_p^\times$ be smooth of order $n$, let $C=\RS[\F_p,H_n,\rho n]$, and let $\eta\in(0,1-\rho)$.  Assume the maximum defining $\mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta)$ in \cref{def:qprofile} is nonempty, let $M^*$ be a divisor attaining it, and put $N^*=n/M^*$.  Suppose
\[
        p>(2(\rho N^*+1))^{N^*/2}.
\]
Then the exact quotient-floor lower bound is
\[
        \emca(C,1-\rho-\eta)
        \ge
        \frac{\Acl(N^*,\rho N^*+1)}{p}.
\]
If moreover $N^*\to\infty$ along the sequence, then
\[
        \Acl(N^*,\rho N^*+1)
        =
        2^{(\beta(\rho)/\HH(\rho))\mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta)(1-o(1))}.
\]
Consequently, along sequences with $N^*\to\infty$, if $\emca(C,1-\rho-\eta)\le n^A/p$ at such a prime, then
\[
        \mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta)
        \le
        \frac{\HH(\rho)}{\beta(\rho)}A\log_2n\,(1+o(1)).
\]
For bounded $N^*$ the exact bound stands while the profile is $O(1)$, so the consequence is vacuous there.  At the deployed rates $1/2,1/4,1/8,1/16$, the constants $\HH(\rho)/\beta(\rho)$ are approximately $1.262$, $1.082$, $1.024$, and $1.009$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
A divisor qualifying for \cref{def:qprofile} satisfies $M^*>\eta n$, equivalently $1/N^*>
\eta$.  The radius $1-\rho-1/N^*$ is therefore below $1-\rho-\eta$, and bad slopes at the former radius remain bad at the latter by monotonicity in \cref{def:mca}.  Applying \cref{prop:qfloor} at quotient order $N^*$ gives at least $\Acl(N^*,\rho N^*+1)$ bad slopes, which is the exact bound.  If $N^*\to\infty$, then
\[
        \mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta)
        =\log_2\binom{N^*-1}{\rho N^*}
        =\HH(\rho)N^*(1-o(1)),
        \qquad
        \log_2\Acl(N^*,\rho N^*+1)=\beta(\rho)N^*(1-o(1)),
\]
by Stirling and \cref{thm:exactcount}, which gives the asymptotic identity and the displayed necessary condition.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Every-prime polynomial lower bounds at logarithmic gap]\label{cor:logfailure}
In the setting of \cref{thm:qnecessity}, with $C=\RS[\F_p,H_n,\rho n]$, let $N'=N'(n)$ be a qualifying quotient order with $N'=(1-o(1))c\log_2 n$ and $1/N'>1/(c\log_2 n)$.  For every prime $p\equiv1\pmod n$ with
\[
        \log_2p\ge \frac c2(1+o(1))\log_2 n\cdot\log_2(c\log_2 n),
\]
\[
        \emca\left(C,1-\rho-\frac1{c\log_2n}\right)
        \ge
        \frac{n^{\beta(\rho)c(1-o(1))}}{p}.
\]
Thus, above the quotient norm threshold, a gap of order $1/\log n$ carries polynomially many quotient-periodic bad slopes of degree $\beta(\rho)c(1-o(1))$ --- any prescribed polynomial degree once the constant $c$ is large.  Superpolynomial counts require $c\to\infty$, i.e.\ gaps of order $o(1/\log n)$.
\end{corollary}

\begin{remark}[Margin against the conjectured positive half]\label{rem:margin}
\Cref{thm:qnecessity} gives the strongest value-counting attack available from the quotient rigidity theory.  If $\mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta)\le B_Q\log_2 n$, the attack produces $n^{(\beta/\HH)B_Q(1-o(1))}$ bad slopes, while a target upper bound of the form $n^{B_Q}/p$ would still survive by the margin
\[
        \HH(\rho)-\beta(\rho)=
        \begin{cases}
        \frac12\bigl(2\HH(\rho)-\HH(2\rho)-2\rho\bigr), & 0<\rho<1/3,\\[1mm]
        \HH(\rho)-\frac12\log_2 3, & 1/3\le\rho\le 2/3,\\[1mm]
        \frac12\bigl(2\HH(\rho)-\HH(2(1-\rho))-2(1-\rho)\bigr), & 2/3<\rho<1,
        \end{cases}
\]
which is positive for $0<\rho<1$: the outer branches equal $f(\min(\rho,1-\rho))$ for $f(\delta)=\HH(\delta)-\frac12\HH(2\delta)-\delta$, which has $f(0)=0$ and $f'(\delta)=\log_2\frac{1-\delta}{1-2\delta}\ge0$, while $\HH(\rho)\ge\HH(1/3)>\frac12\log_23$ on the middle branch.  The margin is $\rho^2/(2\ln2)(1+O(\rho))$ as $\rho\to0$ and $(1-\rho)^2/(2\ln2)(1+O(1-\rho))$ as $\rho\to1$.  Numerically this margin is approximately $0.2075,0.0613,0.0129,0.0030$ at the deployed rates $1/2,1/4,1/8,1/16$.  Any refutation of the positive half beyond this quotient attack must therefore produce, at some quotient scale $N'$, at least $2^{(\HH(\rho)-\beta(\rho))N'(1-o(1))}$ additional bad slopes outside the characteristic-zero quotient image: collision-borne or non-quotient slopes of the kind isolated in \cref{prob:perfiber}.  The empirical frontier in V13--V14 is consistent with this division.
\end{remark}

\subsection{A concrete cap for challenge-size prime fields}

\begin{proposition}[Grand-challenge cap below roughly $150$--$162$ bits]\label{prop:prize}
Let $\rho\in\{1/2,1/4,1/8,1/16\}$, let $D\le\F_p^\times$ be smooth of order $n$ with $64\mid n$, and let $C=\RS[\F_p,D,\rho n]$.  Let $R(\rho)$ be the total reach in the table below.  For every prime $p\le 2^{R(\rho)}$,
\[
        \emca\left(C,1-\rho-\frac1{64}\right)>2^{-128},
        \qquad
        \delta_C^*(2^{-128})<1-\rho-\frac1{64}.
\]
In particular, the conclusion holds uniformly for all four displayed rates for every $p\le2^{150}$.
\begin{center}\small
\begin{tabular}{cccccc}

819	
820	\begin{proof}
821	Fix $(a,b)$.  By \cref{lem:dsh} on $Q$, the first hypothesis gives an $h$-set $B\subseteq Q$ with $e_1(B)=a$.  Since $N$ is even, $-1\in Q$ and the squaring map on $Q$ is two-to-one onto the index-two subgroup $Q^2$ of order $N/2$; let $A_B=Q^2\setminus B^2$, of size at least $N/2-h$.  By \cref{lem:dsh} on $A_B$, the second hypothesis gives an $r$-set $R\subseteq A_B$ with $\sum_{y\in R}y=e_2(B)-b$.  For each $y\in R$ choose $x_y\in Q$ with $x_y^2=y$; since $y\notin B^2$, neither $x_y$ nor $-x_y$ lies in $B$, and pairs over distinct $y$ are disjoint (their elements square to distinct values).  Put $S=B\sqcup\bigsqcup_{y\in R}\{x_y,-x_y\}$, of size $h+2r=\ell$.  Each antipodal pair has $e_1=0$ and $e_2=-x_y^2=-y$; pairs of pairs and pair-versus-$B$ cross terms cancel ($e_2$ of a disjoint union is $e_2+e_2'+e_1e_1'$, and all the pair blocks have $e_1=0$).  Hence
822	\[
823	        e_1(S)=e_1(B)=a,\qquad e_2(S)=e_2(B)-\sum_{y\in R}y=b .
824	\]
825	For the constant, set $h=\alpha N+O(1)$, $r=(\rho-\alpha)N/2+O(1)$: the two hypotheses read $p\le\alpha(1-\alpha)N^2$ and $p\le(\rho-\alpha)(1-\rho-\alpha)N^2/4$, and the balance equation $4\alpha(1-\alpha)=(\rho-\alpha)(1-\rho-\alpha)=\rho(1-\rho)-\alpha+\alpha^2$ simplifies to $5\alpha(1-\alpha)=\rho(1-\rho)$: the balanced value is exactly $\alpha(1-\alpha)=\rho(1-\rho)/5$.
826	\end{proof}
827	
828	\begin{corollary}[Every slack-two monomial line fails at the root barrier, at every prime]\label{cor:twomomentmca}
829	Let $a=n/N$, $a\mid k$, $\ell=\rho N+2$, with the hypotheses of \cref{thm:twomoment} satisfiable at this $\ell$ (in particular $N\ge(1+o(1))\sqrt{5p/\rho(1-\rho)}$).  Then for \emph{every} base $c\in\F_p$, the line
830	\[
831	        u_z\ =\ x^{k+2a}+c\,x^{k+a}+z\,x^{k}
832	\]
833	has every slope $z$ support-wise MCA-bad at radius $1-\rho-2/N$: error one on the whole slack-two monomial family, at every prime.
834	\end{corollary}
835	
836	\begin{proof}
837	\Cref{thm:twomoment} supplies $A\subseteq Q$, $|A|=\ell$, with $(-e_1(A),e_2(A))=(c,z)$; \cref{lem:tlocator} turns $A$ into an agreement set for $u_z$, and condition (ii) holds as always via $g=x^k$.  (Parity of $\ell-h$ is absorbed in the $O(1)$'s.)
838	\end{proof}
839	
840	\begin{remark}[Ledger]\label{rem:twomomentledger}
841	\Cref{thm:twomoment} strengthens, at slack two, both ends of the unconditional ladder: \cref{thm:descent} covers only the diagonal base $c=0$ (at constant $2$ in place of $\sqrt5$), and \cref{thm:ladder} reaches smaller $N$ only with thresholds in $p$.  It is not the entropy-scale result: the conjectural $t=2$ target asks for coverage when $\log_2p\lesssim\tfrac12\HH(\rho)N$, far beyond $p\asymp N^2$; \cref{thm:certificate} identifies the exact finite nonvanishing problem that going further requires.  Verified: at $(p,N,h,r)=(31,30,2,3)$, $\ell=8$, the construction of \cref{thm:twomoment} built witnesses for all $961$ targets $(a,b)\in\F_{31}^2$ with zero failures, and exhaustive enumeration of all $\binom{30}8$ subsets confirms $\Psi_2$ is onto (\cref{app:verify}).
842	\end{remark}
843	
844	\section{The failure ladder, I: the free pool}\label{sec:pool}
845	
846	One lemma drives all the equidistribution-based failure results.  For a subgroup $K\le\F_p^\times$ of order $d$ and $\vec s\in\F_p^t$ write $S_K(\vec s)=\sum_{\kappa\in K}e_p\big(\sum_{m\le t}s_m\kappa^m\big)$.
847	
848	\begin{lemma}[Free pool]\label{lem:pool}
849	Let $t\ge1$, and suppose $K\le Q\le\F_p^\times$, $|K|=d$, $|Q|=N$, satisfy the equidistribution hypothesis
850	\[
851	        \max_{\vec s\ne0}\,|S_K(\vec s)|\ \le\ d\,p^{-\nu}
852	        \qquad\text{for some }\nu>0 .
853	\]
854	Put $C=\lceil(t-\log_pd)/\nu\rceil+6$ and suppose $d\ge4C^2$ and $p^{2\nu}\ge2$.  Then for every $\ell$ with $C\le\ell\le N-d$ and every $\vec v\in\F_p^t$ there is $A\subseteq Q$ with $|A|=\ell$ and $p_m(A)=v_m$ for all $m\le t$: all power-sum fibers are nonempty.  In particular $V_t(Q,\rho N+t)=\F_p$ and, by \cref{thm:slackt}, the MCA error at radius $1-\rho-t/N$ is one.
855	\end{lemma}
856	
857	\begin{proof}
858	Fix $\vec v$.  Choose any $B_0\subseteq Q\setminus K$ with $|B_0|=\ell-C$ (possible since $\ell-C\le N-d$) and set $\vec w=\vec v-(p_1(B_0),\dots,p_t(B_0))$.  For $j\ge2$ let $T_j(\vec w)=\#\{(x_1,\dots,x_j)\in K^j:\ \sum_ix_i^m=w_m\ \forall m\le t\}$.  Orthogonality gives $T_j(\vec w)=p^{-t}\sum_{\vec s}S_K(\vec s)^je_p(-\langle\vec s,\vec w\rangle)$, and the $t$-dimensional Parseval identity reads $\sum_{\vec s}|S_K(\vec s)|^2=p^t\,\#\{(\kappa,\kappa'):\kappa^m=\kappa'^m\ \forall m\le t\}=p^td$, since the $m=1$ equation already forces $\kappa=\kappa'$.  Hence
859	\[
860	        \Big|T_j(\vec w)-\frac{d^{\,j}}{p^{t}}\Big|
861	        \ \le\ \big(\max_{\vec s\ne0}|S_K(\vec s)|\big)^{j-2}\cdot p^{-t}\sum_{\vec s\ne0}|S_K(\vec s)|^{2}
862	        \ \le\ \big(dp^{-\nu}\big)^{j-2}d .
863	\]
864	For $j\in\{C-2,C\}$ the main term dominates by a factor $2$: $d^{\,j}/p^t\ge2(dp^{-\nu})^{j-2}d$ is equivalent to $d\,p^{\nu(j-2)}\ge2p^{t}$, and $j-2\ge C-4\ge(t-\log_pd)/\nu+2$ gives $dp^{\nu(j-2)}\ge p^{t+2\nu}\ge2p^t$.  So $T_j(\vec w)\in[d^{\,j}/(2p^t),\,2d^{\,j}/p^t]$ for these $j$.  Tuples counted by $T_C(\vec w)$ with two equal coordinates number at most $\binom C2\sum_{y\in K}T_{C-2}(\vec w-(2y,2y^2,\dots,2y^t))\le C^2d^{\,C-1}/p^t$, which is at most $\tfrac12\cdot d^{\,C}/(2p^t)$ once $d\ge4C^2$.  Some tuple therefore has $C$ pairwise distinct coordinates in $K$ with the prescribed power sums; adjoin $B_0$.
865	\end{proof}
866	
867	Three exponential-sum inputs instantiate the hypothesis.
868	
869	\begin{theorem}[Conditional polynomial range from a free-pool input]\label{thm:ladder}
870	Let $D\le\F_p^\times$ be smooth of order $n$, $C=\RS[\F_p,D,\rho n]$, and fix a divisor scale $N\mid n$ with quotient subgroup $Q$ of order $N$.  Assume that $Q$ contains a subgroup $K$ of order $d$ for which the exponential-sum hypothesis of \cref{lem:pool} holds for the relevant degree $t$, with a saving $\nu>0$, and that $\ell=\rho N+t$ satisfies
871	\[
872	        C(t,d,p,\nu)\le \ell\le N-d,
873	\]
874	where $C(t,d,p,\nu)=\lceil(t-\log_p d)/\nu\rceil+6$ is the pool size from \cref{lem:pool}.  Then
875	\[
876	        \emca(C,1-\rho-t/N)=1 .
877	\]
878	In particular, the following are valid wherever the cited exponential-sum estimates supply the displayed hypothesis with the quoted savings.
879	\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*),leftmargin=2.2em]

300	For every subset $A$ of the quotient $H/K\setminus\{C_0\}$ of size $k/M$, let $U_A\subset H$ be the union of the $K$-cosets in $A$.  Since each $K$-coset is a fiber of $x\mapsto x^M$, its locator has the form $X^M-\alpha$.  Hence
301	\[
302	        L_A(X)=\prod_{x\in U_A}(X-x)=\prod_{\alpha\in A'}(X^M-\alpha)
303	        =X^k+c_1X^{k-M}+c_2X^{k-2M}+\cdots .
304	\]
305	Thus $X^k-L_A(X)$ has degree at most $k-M$.  Define
306	\[
307	        P_A(X)=Y(X)-L_T(X)L_A(X)=L_T(X)(X^k-L_A(X)).
308	\]
309	Since $\deg L_T=\sigma<M$, we have $\deg P_A<k$, so $P_A$ is a codeword.  On $T\cup U_A$, either $L_T$ or $L_A$ vanishes, so $P_A=Y$.  The agreement set has size $\sigma+k$.  Distinct $A$ give distinct locators and distinct codewords.
310	\end{proof}
311	
312	\begin{corollary}[The one-scale ambient-$q$ conjecture is false]\label{cor:one-scale-false}
313	Fix a deployed rate $\rho=2^{-a}$ and $\eps>0$.  There are infinitely many smooth prime-field domains $H\le\F_p^\times$ with $|H|=n$ and
314	\[
315	        p=2^{\Theta(\sqrt n)}
316	\]
317	such that at radius
318	\[
319	        1-\rho-(1+\eps+o(1))\taustar(\rho,p)
320	\]
321	the list size is at least
322	\[
323	        p^{1/(2(1+\eps))-o(1)}=2^{\Theta(\sqrt n)},
324	\]
325	which is superpolynomial in $n$.
326	\end{corollary}
327	
328	\begin{proof}
329	Take $n=2^m$.  By the prime number theorem in arithmetic progressions in a Siegel--Walfisz range \cite{Dav00}, there are primes $p\equiv1\pmod n$ with $\log_2p=\Theta(\sqrt n)$ for all large $n$ along a suitable sequence.  Let $H\le\F_p^\times$ have order $n$.  Since $p$ is prime, there is no proper subfield obstruction.
330	
331	Let $\sigma=\lceil(1+\eps)\taustar(\rho,p)n\rceil$.  Because $\taustar(\rho,p)=(\HH(\rho)+o(1))/\log_2p$, we have $\sigma=\Theta(\sqrt n)$.  Let $M$ be the least power of two larger than $\sigma$.  For large $n$, $M\mid k=\rho n$ and $M\le2\sigma$.  \Cref{thm:qcore} gives
332	\[
333	        L\ge \binom{n/M-1}{k/M}.
334	\]
335	By Stirling,
336	\[
337	        \log_2 L\ge \frac{n}{M}\HH(\rho)-o(n/M)
338	        \ge \frac{\HH(\rho)+o(1)}{2(1+\eps)\taustar(\rho,p)}
339	        =\left(\frac1{2(1+\eps)}-o(1)\right)\log_2p .
340	\]
341	\end{proof}
342	
343	\begin{remark}[Corrected reserve]\label{rem:corrected-reserve}
344	For a list bound $n^B$, the quotient-core family at $\sigma=Cn/\log_2n$ has size about $n^{\HH(\rho)/C}$ on the grid.  Thus a theorem with list bound $n^B$ must have $C\gtrsim \HH(\rho)/B$ at the second scale.  For unspecified polynomial list size, the necessary scale is $\sigma=\Omega(n/\log n)$; for fixed exponent $B$, the constant matters.
345	\end{remark}
346	
347	\begin{definition}[Quotient-core profile]\label{def:qprofile}
348	For a smooth domain $H$ of order $n$ and $k=\rho n$,
349	\[
350	        \mathcal Q_H(\eta)\ :=\ \max_{\substack{M\mid\gcd(n,k),\ \lceil\eta n\rceil<M\\ k/M\le n/M-1}}\ \log_2\binom{n/M-1}{k/M}
351	\]
352	($-\infty$ over the empty set): the logarithmic size of the largest quotient-core list still active at gap $\eta$.  Correspondingly, every quotient contribution of the form $2^{(\beta(\rho)/\HH(\rho))\mathcal Q_H(\eta)(1+o(1))}$ below is read as zero when the defining maximum is empty.
353	\end{definition}
354	
355	\begin{conjecture}[Quotient-profile corrected list entropy gap]\label{conj:listprofile}
356	Let $C_n=\RS[\F_{q_n},H_n,\rho n]$ with $\mathrm{poly}(n)\le q_n\le2^{o(n)}$ and $q_{D,n}$ the generated-field size, and fix a list target $n^B$.  Necessary for $\Lst(C_n,1-\rho-\eta_n)\le n^B$ is
357	\[
358	        \eta_n\ \ge\ (1-o(1))\,\taustar(\rho,q_{D,n}),
359	        \qquad
360	        \mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)\ \le\ B\log_2n+O(\log\log n)
361	\]
362	(\cref{cor:genfield-pigeonhole,thm:qcore}); the conjectural positive half is that, after fixed slack in both inequalities, the conditions are sufficient.  On dyadic domains at the deployed rates the scale form is $\eta_n\gtrsim\max\{\taustar(\rho,q_{D,n}),\,\Theta_{\rho,B}(1/\log n)\}$.
363	\end{conjecture}
364	
365	\section{Characteristic-zero inverse quotient theorem}\label{sec:upstairs}
366	
367	We now prove that, over characteristic zero, the quotient-core phenomenon is the entire prefix-fiber structure for $2$-power roots.  This is the toric Manin--Mumford/torsion-coset part of the program (compare the torsion-coset theorem \cite{Laurent84} and the theory of vanishing sums of roots of unity \cite{CJ76,LL00}), but for $2$-primary roots it has an elementary proof.
368	
369	Let $n=2^m$, let $\zeta=e^{2\pi i/n}$, and identify $\mu_n=\langle\zeta\rangle$ with the complex $n$-th roots of unity.  For $h:\mu_n\to\Z$, define
370	\[
371	        \widehat h(j)=\sum_{x\in\mu_n}h(x)x^j\in\Z[\zeta].
372	\]
373	
374	\begin{lemma}[Antipodal identity]\label{lem:antipodal}
375	Let $n'=2^r$ with $r\ge1$, and let $g:\mu_{n'}\to\Z$.  If
376	\[
377	        \sum_{y\in\mu_{n'}}g(y)y=0,
378	\]
379	then $g(y)=g(-y)$ for every $y\in\mu_{n'}$.
380	\end{lemma}
381	
382	\begin{proof}
383	Write $y=\zeta_{n'}^a$.  The sum becomes
384	\[
385	        \sum_{a=0}^{n'/2-1}\big(g(\zeta_{n'}^a)-g(-\zeta_{n'}^a)\big)\zeta_{n'}^a.
386	\]
387	The powers $1,\zeta_{n'},\ldots,\zeta_{n'}^{n'/2-1}$ are linearly independent over $\Q$ because $\Phi_{n'}(X)=X^{n'/2}+1$.  Hence every coefficient difference is zero.
388	\end{proof}
389	
390	\begin{theorem}[Inverse quotient theorem over $\C$]\label{thm:upstairs}
391	Let $n=2^m$, $1\le\sigma<n$, and let $M_0$ be the least power of two with $M_0>\sigma$.  Let $S,T\subseteq\mu_n$ have the same cardinality and satisfy
392	\[
393	        e_j(S)=e_j(T)\qquad(1\le j\le\sigma)
394	\]
395	over $\C$.  Then $S\triangle T$ is a union of cosets of the subgroup $\mu_{M_0}\le\mu_n$.  Equivalently, $\mathbf 1_S-\mathbf 1_T$ is constant on $\mu_{M_0}$-cosets.  Conversely, every such quotient rearrangement preserves all $e_j$ for $1\le j<M_0$.
396	\end{theorem}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	By Newton identities in characteristic zero, equality of $e_1,\ldots,e_\sigma$ and equality of cardinalities imply equality of the first $\sigma$ power sums.  Thus, for $h=\mathbf 1_S-\mathbf 1_T$,
400	\[
401	        \widehat h(j)=0\qquad(1\le j\le\sigma).
402	\]
403	It is enough to use the dyadic frequencies.  Let $M_0=2^a$.  Since $2^{a-1}\le\sigma$, we have
404	\[
405	        \widehat h(1)=\widehat h(2)=\cdots=\widehat h(2^{a-1})=0
406	\]
407	at the listed dyadic powers.
408	
409	We prove by induction that vanishing at $1,2,\ldots,2^i$ in this dyadic sense forces invariance under $\mu_{2^{i+1}}$.  For $i=0$, $\widehat h(1)=0$ and \Cref{lem:antipodal} give invariance under $\mu_2$.  Suppose $h$ is invariant under $\mu_{2^i}$ and $\widehat h(2^i)=0$.  The map $x\mapsto x^{2^i}$ sends $\mu_n$ onto $\mu_{n/2^i}$ with fibers the $\mu_{2^i}$-cosets.  Let $h^\ast(y)$ be the common value on the fiber above $y$.  Then
410	\[
411	        0=\widehat h(2^i)=2^i\sum_{y\in\mu_{n/2^i}}h^\ast(y)y.
412	\]
413	By \Cref{lem:antipodal}, $h^\ast(y)=h^\ast(-y)$.  Pulling this identity back says that $h$ is invariant under a primitive $2^{i+1}$-st root of unity, hence under $\mu_{2^{i+1}}$.  Iterating to $i=a-1$ gives $\mu_{M_0}$-invariance.
414	
415	For the converse, if $h$ is constant on $\mu_{M_0}$-cosets, then
416	\[
417	        \widehat h(j)=\sum_{\alpha\mu_{M_0}}h(\alpha)\alpha^j\sum_{\kappa\in\mu_{M_0}}\kappa^j=0
418	\]
419	whenever $M_0\nmid j$, in particular for $1\le j<M_0$.  Newton identities convert the corresponding power-sum preservation into preservation of $e_1,\ldots,e_{M_0-1}$ for equal-size subsets.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\begin{corollary}[Polynomial size of characteristic-zero fibers]\label{cor:upstairs-poly}
423	Every characteristic-zero prefix fiber in $\binom{\mu_n}{s}$ has size at most
424	\[
425	        2^{n/M_0}\le 2^{n/\sigma}.
426	\]
427	If $\sigma\ge Cn/\log_2n$, then every such fiber has size at most $n^{1/C}$.  With fixed density one may sharpen this to
428	\[
429	        2^{(n/M_0)\HH(\rho)+o(n/M_0)}\le n^{\HH(\rho)/C+o(1)}.

For every subset $A$ of the quotient $H/K\setminus\{C_0\}$ of size $k/M$, let $U_A\subset H$ be the union of the $K$-cosets in $A$.  Since each $K$-coset is a fiber of $x\mapsto x^M$, its locator has the form $X^M-\alpha$.  Hence
\[
        L_A(X)=\prod_{x\in U_A}(X-x)=\prod_{\alpha\in A'}(X^M-\alpha)
        =X^k+c_1X^{k-M}+c_2X^{k-2M}+\cdots .
\]
Thus $X^k-L_A(X)$ has degree at most $k-M$.  Define
\[
        P_A(X)=Y(X)-L_T(X)L_A(X)=L_T(X)(X^k-L_A(X)).
\]
Since $\deg L_T=\sigma<M$, we have $\deg P_A<k$, so $P_A$ is a codeword.  On $T\cup U_A$, either $L_T$ or $L_A$ vanishes, so $P_A=Y$.  The agreement set has size $\sigma+k$.  Distinct $A$ give distinct locators and distinct codewords.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[The one-scale ambient-$q$ conjecture is false]\label{cor:one-scale-false}
Fix a deployed rate $\rho=2^{-a}$ and $\eps>0$.  There are infinitely many smooth prime-field domains $H\le\F_p^\times$ with $|H|=n$ and
\[
        p=2^{\Theta(\sqrt n)}
\]
such that at radius
\[
        1-\rho-(1+\eps+o(1))\taustar(\rho,p)
\]
the list size is at least
\[
        p^{1/(2(1+\eps))-o(1)}=2^{\Theta(\sqrt n)},
\]
which is superpolynomial in $n$.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Take $n=2^m$.  By the prime number theorem in arithmetic progressions in a Siegel--Walfisz range \cite{Dav00}, there are primes $p\equiv1\pmod n$ with $\log_2p=\Theta(\sqrt n)$ for all large $n$ along a suitable sequence.  Let $H\le\F_p^\times$ have order $n$.  Since $p$ is prime, there is no proper subfield obstruction.

Let $\sigma=\lceil(1+\eps)\taustar(\rho,p)n\rceil$.  Because $\taustar(\rho,p)=(\HH(\rho)+o(1))/\log_2p$, we have $\sigma=\Theta(\sqrt n)$.  Let $M$ be the least power of two larger than $\sigma$.  For large $n$, $M\mid k=\rho n$ and $M\le2\sigma$.  \Cref{thm:qcore} gives
\[
        L\ge \binom{n/M-1}{k/M}.
\]
By Stirling,
\[
        \log_2 L\ge \frac{n}{M}\HH(\rho)-o(n/M)
        \ge \frac{\HH(\rho)+o(1)}{2(1+\eps)\taustar(\rho,p)}
        =\left(\frac1{2(1+\eps)}-o(1)\right)\log_2p .
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Corrected reserve]\label{rem:corrected-reserve}
For a list bound $n^B$, the quotient-core family at $\sigma=Cn/\log_2n$ has size about $n^{\HH(\rho)/C}$ on the grid.  Thus a theorem with list bound $n^B$ must have $C\gtrsim \HH(\rho)/B$ at the second scale.  For unspecified polynomial list size, the necessary scale is $\sigma=\Omega(n/\log n)$; for fixed exponent $B$, the constant matters.
\end{remark}

\begin{definition}[Quotient-core profile]\label{def:qprofile}
For a smooth domain $H$ of order $n$ and $k=\rho n$,
\[
        \mathcal Q_H(\eta)\ :=\ \max_{\substack{M\mid\gcd(n,k),\ \lceil\eta n\rceil<M\\ k/M\le n/M-1}}\ \log_2\binom{n/M-1}{k/M}
\]
($-\infty$ over the empty set): the logarithmic size of the largest quotient-core list still active at gap $\eta$.  Correspondingly, every quotient contribution of the form $2^{(\beta(\rho)/\HH(\rho))\mathcal Q_H(\eta)(1+o(1))}$ below is read as zero when the defining maximum is empty.
\end{definition}

\begin{conjecture}[Quotient-profile corrected list entropy gap]\label{conj:listprofile}
Let $C_n=\RS[\F_{q_n},H_n,\rho n]$ with $\mathrm{poly}(n)\le q_n\le2^{o(n)}$ and $q_{D,n}$ the generated-field size, and fix a list target $n^B$.  Necessary for $\Lst(C_n,1-\rho-\eta_n)\le n^B$ is
\[
        \eta_n\ \ge\ (1-o(1))\,\taustar(\rho,q_{D,n}),
        \qquad
        \mathcal Q_{H_n}(\eta_n)\ \le\ B\log_2n+O(\log\log n)
\]
(\cref{cor:genfield-pigeonhole,thm:qcore}); the conjectural positive half is that, after fixed slack in both inequalities, the conditions are sufficient.  On dyadic domains at the deployed rates the scale form is $\eta_n\gtrsim\max\{\taustar(\rho,q_{D,n}),\,\Theta_{\rho,B}(1/\log n)\}$.
\end{conjecture}

\section{Characteristic-zero inverse quotient theorem}\label{sec:upstairs}

We now prove that, over characteristic zero, the quotient-core phenomenon is the entire prefix-fiber structure for $2$-power roots.  This is the toric Manin--Mumford/torsion-coset part of the program (compare the torsion-coset theorem \cite{Laurent84} and the theory of vanishing sums of roots of unity \cite{CJ76,LL00}), but for $2$-primary roots it has an elementary proof.

Let $n=2^m$, let $\zeta=e^{2\pi i/n}$, and identify $\mu_n=\langle\zeta\rangle$ with the complex $n$-th roots of unity.  For $h:\mu_n\to\Z$, define
\[
        \widehat h(j)=\sum_{x\in\mu_n}h(x)x^j\in\Z[\zeta].
\]

\begin{lemma}[Antipodal identity]\label{lem:antipodal}
Let $n'=2^r$ with $r\ge1$, and let $g:\mu_{n'}\to\Z$.  If
\[
        \sum_{y\in\mu_{n'}}g(y)y=0,
\]
then $g(y)=g(-y)$ for every $y\in\mu_{n'}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Write $y=\zeta_{n'}^a$.  The sum becomes
\[
        \sum_{a=0}^{n'/2-1}\big(g(\zeta_{n'}^a)-g(-\zeta_{n'}^a)\big)\zeta_{n'}^a.
\]
The powers $1,\zeta_{n'},\ldots,\zeta_{n'}^{n'/2-1}$ are linearly independent over $\Q$ because $\Phi_{n'}(X)=X^{n'/2}+1$.  Hence every coefficient difference is zero.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Inverse quotient theorem over $\C$]\label{thm:upstairs}
Let $n=2^m$, $1\le\sigma<n$, and let $M_0$ be the least power of two with $M_0>\sigma$.  Let $S,T\subseteq\mu_n$ have the same cardinality and satisfy
\[
        e_j(S)=e_j(T)\qquad(1\le j\le\sigma)
\]
over $\C$.  Then $S\triangle T$ is a union of cosets of the subgroup $\mu_{M_0}\le\mu_n$.  Equivalently, $\mathbf 1_S-\mathbf 1_T$ is constant on $\mu_{M_0}$-cosets.  Conversely, every such quotient rearrangement preserves all $e_j$ for $1\le j<M_0$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
By Newton identities in characteristic zero, equality of $e_1,\ldots,e_\sigma$ and equality of cardinalities imply equality of the first $\sigma$ power sums.  Thus, for $h=\mathbf 1_S-\mathbf 1_T$,
\[
        \widehat h(j)=0\qquad(1\le j\le\sigma).
\]
It is enough to use the dyadic frequencies.  Let $M_0=2^a$.  Since $2^{a-1}\le\sigma$, we have
\[
        \widehat h(1)=\widehat h(2)=\cdots=\widehat h(2^{a-1})=0
\]
at the listed dyadic powers.

We prove by induction that vanishing at $1,2,\ldots,2^i$ in this dyadic sense forces invariance under $\mu_{2^{i+1}}$.  For $i=0$, $\widehat h(1)=0$ and \Cref{lem:antipodal} give invariance under $\mu_2$.  Suppose $h$ is invariant under $\mu_{2^i}$ and $\widehat h(2^i)=0$.  The map $x\mapsto x^{2^i}$ sends $\mu_n$ onto $\mu_{n/2^i}$ with fibers the $\mu_{2^i}$-cosets.  Let $h^\ast(y)$ be the common value on the fiber above $y$.  Then
\[
        0=\widehat h(2^i)=2^i\sum_{y\in\mu_{n/2^i}}h^\ast(y)y.
\]
By \Cref{lem:antipodal}, $h^\ast(y)=h^\ast(-y)$.  Pulling this identity back says that $h$ is invariant under a primitive $2^{i+1}$-st root of unity, hence under $\mu_{2^{i+1}}$.  Iterating to $i=a-1$ gives $\mu_{M_0}$-invariance.

For the converse, if $h$ is constant on $\mu_{M_0}$-cosets, then
\[
        \widehat h(j)=\sum_{\alpha\mu_{M_0}}h(\alpha)\alpha^j\sum_{\kappa\in\mu_{M_0}}\kappa^j=0
\]
whenever $M_0\nmid j$, in particular for $1\le j<M_0$.  Newton identities convert the corresponding power-sum preservation into preservation of $e_1,\ldots,e_{M_0-1}$ for equal-size subsets.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Polynomial size of characteristic-zero fibers]\label{cor:upstairs-poly}
Every characteristic-zero prefix fiber in $\binom{\mu_n}{s}$ has size at most
\[
        2^{n/M_0}\le 2^{n/\sigma}.
\]
If $\sigma\ge Cn/\log_2n$, then every such fiber has size at most $n^{1/C}$.  With fixed density one may sharpen this to
\[
        2^{(n/M_0)\HH(\rho)+o(n/M_0)}\le n^{\HH(\rho)/C+o(1)}.

442	\[
443	        F_{S,T}(X)=\sum_{a\in A}X^a-\sum_{b\in B}X^b\in\Z[X],
444	        \qquad \deg F_{S,T}<n.
445	\]
446	Equality of the first $\sigma$ power sums in $\F_p$ is exactly
447	\[
448	        F_{S,T}(\omega^j)=0\qquad(1\le j\le\sigma).
449	\]
450	If $p>\sigma$, this is equivalent to equality of $e_1,\ldots,e_\sigma$ in $\F_p$.
451	
452	\begin{lemma}[Conjugate-prime amplification]\label{lem:galois-amp}
453	Let $F\in\Z[X]$ have degree $<n$, and assume $F(\zeta)\ne0$.  If
454	\[
455	        F(\omega^j)=0
456	\]
457	for every odd $j\le\sigma$, then
458	\[
459	        p^{\lceil\sigma/2\rceil}\mid \big|\Norm_{\Q(\zeta)/\Q}(F(\zeta))\big|.
460	\]
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	Let $\mathfrak p_\omega\subset\Z[\zeta]$ be the degree-one prime ideal corresponding to $\zeta\mapsto\omega$.  For odd $j$, the automorphism $\tau_j:\zeta\mapsto\zeta^j$ is in $\operatorname{Gal}(\Q(\zeta)/\Q)$.  The congruence $F(\omega^j)=0$ says $\tau_j(F(\zeta))\in\mathfrak p_\omega$, equivalently
465	\[
466	        F(\zeta)\in\tau_j^{-1}\mathfrak p_\omega.
467	\]
468	For distinct odd $j$ modulo $n$, these are distinct prime ideals above $p$, each of norm $p$.  There are $\lceil\sigma/2\rceil$ odd integers in $[1,\sigma]$.  Their product divides the principal ideal $(F(\zeta))$, yielding the norm divisibility.
469	\end{proof}
470	
471	The quotient factors must be treated uniformly.  For $M=2^b\mid n$ define
472	\[
473	        Q_M(X)=1+X^{n/M}+X^{2n/M}+\cdots+X^{(M-1)n/M}=\frac{X^n-1}{X^{n/M}-1}.
474	\]
475	Divisibility by $Q_M$ means that the coefficients of $F$ are constant on residue classes modulo $n/M$, i.e. on cosets of the subgroup of order $M$.
476	
477	\begin{theorem}[Galois-amplified no-collision bound]\label{thm:no-collision}
478	There is an absolute constant $C_1$ with the following property.  Let $n=2^m$, $1\le\sigma<n$, $p\equiv1\pmod n$, and $p>\sigma$.  Suppose
479	\[
480	        p>\exp\!\left(C_1\frac{n\log(2n)}{\sigma}\right).
481	\]
482	Then every equality
483	\[
484	        (e_1(S),\ldots,e_\sigma(S))=(e_1(T),\ldots,e_\sigma(T))\quad\text{in }\F_p^\sigma
485	\]
486	for equal-size subsets $S,T\subseteq H=\langle\omega\rangle$ is quotient-periodic: after identifying $H$ with $\mu_n$, the symmetric difference $S\triangle T$ is a union of cosets of a subgroup of order $M>\sigma$.
487	\end{theorem}
488	
489	\begin{proof}
490	Use power sums, which are equivalent because $p>\sigma$.  Let $F=F_{S,T}$.  If $F$ is divisible by $Q_M$ for some $M>\sigma$, then $Q_M(\zeta^j)=0$ for $1\le j\le\sigma$, and this is exactly a quotient-periodic characteristic-zero collision, handled by \Cref{thm:upstairs}.
491	
492	Assume no such quotient divisor with $M>\sigma$ exists.  Let $M$ be the largest power of two such that $F=Q_MG$ with $G\in\Z[X]$; possibly $M=1$.  Then $M\le\sigma$.  Put $\xi=\zeta^M$, a primitive $(n/M)$-th root of unity.  Maximality of $M$ implies $G(\xi)\ne0$; otherwise $\Phi_{n/M}$ would divide $G$ and hence $Q_{2M}$ would divide $F$.
493	
494	For every $\ell\le\sigma/M$, the finite-field equation at $j=M\ell$ gives
495	\[
496	        0=F(\omega^{M\ell})=Q_M(\omega^{M\ell})G((\omega^M)^\ell)=M\,G((\omega^M)^\ell),
497	\]
498	and $M$ is invertible modulo the odd prime $p$.  Applying \Cref{lem:galois-amp} in the smaller field $\Q(\xi)$, with $n$ replaced by $n/M$ and $\sigma$ replaced by $\lfloor\sigma/M\rfloor$, yields
499	\[
500	        p^{\lceil\sigma/(3M)\rceil}\le \big|\Norm_{\Q(\xi)/\Q}(G(\xi))\big|.
501	\]
502	The harmless factor $3$ absorbs the floor and the restriction to odd $\ell$.
503	
504	Since the coefficients of $F$ lie in $\{-1,0,1\}$ and $F=Q_MG$ with $\deg F<n$, the coefficients of $G$ are also in $\{-1,0,1\}$.  Hence every complex conjugate of $G(\xi)$ has absolute value at most $n/M$, and therefore
505	\[
506	        \big|\Norm_{\Q(\xi)/\Q}(G(\xi))\big|
507	        \le (2n)^{\varphi(n/M)}
508	        \le (2n)^{n/(2M)}.
509	\]
510	Thus
511	\[
512	        p^{\sigma/(4M)}\le (2n)^{n/(2M)}
513	\]
514	for all large enough parameters, which implies
515	\[
516	        \log p\le 2\frac{n\log(2n)}{\sigma}
517	\]
518	up to an absolute constant.  Choosing $C_1$ larger gives a contradiction.  Therefore the collision must have had a quotient divisor with $M>\sigma$.
519	\end{proof}
520	
521	\begin{corollary}[Monomial-prefix upper bound in quasi-polynomial split prime fields]\label{cor:quasipoly-upper}
522	Let $n=2^m$, $p\equiv1\pmod n$, $H\le\F_p^\times$ have order $n$, and $s=k+\sigma$.  If
523	\[
524	        p>\exp\!\left(C_1\frac{n\log(2n)}{\sigma}\right),
525	\]
526	then every monomial-prefix word has list size at most
527	\[
528	        2^{n/M_0}\le 2^{n/\sigma},
529	\]
530	where $M_0$ is the least power of two greater than $\sigma$.  In particular, if $\sigma\ge C_0n/\log_2n$, then every monomial-prefix list has size at most $n^{1/C_0+o(1)}$ provided
531	\[
532	        p>\exp\big(O_{C_0}((\log n)^2)\big).
533	\]
534	\end{corollary}

536	\begin{proof}

For $S,T\subseteq\mu_n$ with exponent sets $A,B\subseteq\Z/n\Z$, define the signed subset polynomial
\[
        F_{S,T}(X)=\sum_{a\in A}X^a-\sum_{b\in B}X^b\in\Z[X],
        \qquad \deg F_{S,T}<n.
\]
Equality of the first $\sigma$ power sums in $\F_p$ is exactly
\[
        F_{S,T}(\omega^j)=0\qquad(1\le j\le\sigma).
\]
If $p>\sigma$, this is equivalent to equality of $e_1,\ldots,e_\sigma$ in $\F_p$.

\begin{lemma}[Conjugate-prime amplification]\label{lem:galois-amp}
Let $F\in\Z[X]$ have degree $<n$, and assume $F(\zeta)\ne0$.  If
\[
        F(\omega^j)=0
\]
for every odd $j\le\sigma$, then
\[
        p^{\lceil\sigma/2\rceil}\mid \big|\Norm_{\Q(\zeta)/\Q}(F(\zeta))\big|.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let $\mathfrak p_\omega\subset\Z[\zeta]$ be the degree-one prime ideal corresponding to $\zeta\mapsto\omega$.  For odd $j$, the automorphism $\tau_j:\zeta\mapsto\zeta^j$ is in $\operatorname{Gal}(\Q(\zeta)/\Q)$.  The congruence $F(\omega^j)=0$ says $\tau_j(F(\zeta))\in\mathfrak p_\omega$, equivalently
\[
        F(\zeta)\in\tau_j^{-1}\mathfrak p_\omega.
\]
For distinct odd $j$ modulo $n$, these are distinct prime ideals above $p$, each of norm $p$.  There are $\lceil\sigma/2\rceil$ odd integers in $[1,\sigma]$.  Their product divides the principal ideal $(F(\zeta))$, yielding the norm divisibility.
\end{proof}

The quotient factors must be treated uniformly.  For $M=2^b\mid n$ define
\[
        Q_M(X)=1+X^{n/M}+X^{2n/M}+\cdots+X^{(M-1)n/M}=\frac{X^n-1}{X^{n/M}-1}.
\]
Divisibility by $Q_M$ means that the coefficients of $F$ are constant on residue classes modulo $n/M$, i.e. on cosets of the subgroup of order $M$.

\begin{theorem}[Galois-amplified no-collision bound]\label{thm:no-collision}
There is an absolute constant $C_1$ with the following property.  Let $n=2^m$, $1\le\sigma<n$, $p\equiv1\pmod n$, and $p>\sigma$.  Suppose
\[
        p>\exp\!\left(C_1\frac{n\log(2n)}{\sigma}\right).
\]
Then every equality
\[
        (e_1(S),\ldots,e_\sigma(S))=(e_1(T),\ldots,e_\sigma(T))\quad\text{in }\F_p^\sigma
\]
for equal-size subsets $S,T\subseteq H=\langle\omega\rangle$ is quotient-periodic: after identifying $H$ with $\mu_n$, the symmetric difference $S\triangle T$ is a union of cosets of a subgroup of order $M>\sigma$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Use power sums, which are equivalent because $p>\sigma$.  Let $F=F_{S,T}$.  If $F$ is divisible by $Q_M$ for some $M>\sigma$, then $Q_M(\zeta^j)=0$ for $1\le j\le\sigma$, and this is exactly a quotient-periodic characteristic-zero collision, handled by \Cref{thm:upstairs}.

Assume no such quotient divisor with $M>\sigma$ exists.  Let $M$ be the largest power of two such that $F=Q_MG$ with $G\in\Z[X]$; possibly $M=1$.  Then $M\le\sigma$.  Put $\xi=\zeta^M$, a primitive $(n/M)$-th root of unity.  Maximality of $M$ implies $G(\xi)\ne0$; otherwise $\Phi_{n/M}$ would divide $G$ and hence $Q_{2M}$ would divide $F$.

For every $\ell\le\sigma/M$, the finite-field equation at $j=M\ell$ gives
\[
        0=F(\omega^{M\ell})=Q_M(\omega^{M\ell})G((\omega^M)^\ell)=M\,G((\omega^M)^\ell),
\]
and $M$ is invertible modulo the odd prime $p$.  Applying \Cref{lem:galois-amp} in the smaller field $\Q(\xi)$, with $n$ replaced by $n/M$ and $\sigma$ replaced by $\lfloor\sigma/M\rfloor$, yields
\[
        p^{\lceil\sigma/(3M)\rceil}\le \big|\Norm_{\Q(\xi)/\Q}(G(\xi))\big|.
\]
The harmless factor $3$ absorbs the floor and the restriction to odd $\ell$.

Since the coefficients of $F$ lie in $\{-1,0,1\}$ and $F=Q_MG$ with $\deg F<n$, the coefficients of $G$ are also in $\{-1,0,1\}$.  Hence every complex conjugate of $G(\xi)$ has absolute value at most $n/M$, and therefore
\[
        \big|\Norm_{\Q(\xi)/\Q}(G(\xi))\big|
        \le (2n)^{\varphi(n/M)}
        \le (2n)^{n/(2M)}.
\]
Thus
\[
        p^{\sigma/(4M)}\le (2n)^{n/(2M)}
\]
for all large enough parameters, which implies
\[
        \log p\le 2\frac{n\log(2n)}{\sigma}
\]
up to an absolute constant.  Choosing $C_1$ larger gives a contradiction.  Therefore the collision must have had a quotient divisor with $M>\sigma$.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Monomial-prefix upper bound in quasi-polynomial split prime fields]\label{cor:quasipoly-upper}
Let $n=2^m$, $p\equiv1\pmod n$, $H\le\F_p^\times$ have order $n$, and $s=k+\sigma$.  If
\[
        p>\exp\!\left(C_1\frac{n\log(2n)}{\sigma}\right),
\]
then every monomial-prefix word has list size at most
\[
        2^{n/M_0}\le 2^{n/\sigma},
\]
where $M_0$ is the least power of two greater than $\sigma$.  In particular, if $\sigma\ge C_0n/\log_2n$, then every monomial-prefix list has size at most $n^{1/C_0+o(1)}$ provided
\[
        p>\exp\big(O_{C_0}((\log n)^2)\big).
\]
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
By \Cref{thm:no-collision}, a finite-field prefix fiber coincides with a characteristic-zero quotient fiber.  Apply \Cref{cor:upstairs-poly}.  At $\sigma\ge C_0n/\log_2n$, the threshold on $p$ becomes $\exp(O_{C_0}((\log n)^2))$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What the Galois sieve does and does not prove]\label{rem:galois-limit}
A one-prime norm sieve --- the mechanism behind the stable range and the density windows of Part~II --- only gives useful information when $p\gg\binom ns^2\operatorname{poly}(n)$.  \Cref{thm:no-collision} is stronger by a factor $\asymp\sigma$ in the exponent because the equations at odd powers give distinct conjugate prime divisors.  Nevertheless it still misses polynomial fields at the critical reserve $\sigma\asymp n/\log n$: then it needs $p>\exp(O((\log n)^2))$, while polynomial fields have $p=\exp(O(\log n))$.  Closing this last factor of $\log n$ requires a genuinely finite-field local-limit theorem, not a norm-divisor argument.
\end{remark}

\section{The corrected positive conjectures}\label{sec:conjectures}

The lower bounds and the quasi-polynomial-field theorem point to the following two-scale statement.  (For \emph{random} evaluation sets, capacity-achieving list decodability is known \cite{BGM23,GZ23}; the content here is the structured smooth-domain case, where \cref{cor:one-scale-false} shows the random prediction is false as stated.)

\[
        \sigma\ge C\frac{n}{\log_2n}
        \qquad\text{and}\qquad
        \sigma\log_2q\ge (1+\eps)\log_2\binom ns,
\]
then for every $c\in\F_q^\sigma$,
\[
        \#\{S\in\binom Hs:\Phi_\sigma(S)=c\}\le n^B.
\]

content.thinking:
I'm consolidating my understanding of the source material to work through Cycle 95, starting with reconstructing the precise setup for the t=1 case where E equals X minus some α outside D, B has degree 1, and w maps D to the field.

For each slope z at radius δ, I have a witness pair (Q_z, S_z) where Q_z has degree at most k, satisfies Q_z(α) = z, and equals w on the agreement set S_z of size at least k + σ. The noncontainment condition requires that no pair of polynomials A and G of degree less than k can simultaneously represent w/E and -1/(X-α) on S_z.

The core question is counting how many distinct slopes z admit a noncontained exact (k+σ)-agreement witness, which hinges on the constraint that Q_z must interpolate z at α while matching w on a sufficiently large subset of D. A degree-$\le k$ polynomial is uniquely determined by $k+1$ values, so when $Q_z$ agrees with $w$ on $k+\sigma$ points (where $\sigma \ge 1$), it's uniquely determined by any $k+1$ of those points, and the remaining $\sigma-1$ agreements become constraints on $w$. The bad slopes are then exactly those of the form $Q_S(\alpha)$ where $S$ is a $(k+\sigma)$-subset on which $w$ is interpolated by a degree-$\le k$ polynomial $Q_S$ that isn't contained. For noncontainment, I need $A, G \in \F_{<k}[X]$ with $A = w/E$ and $G = -1/(X-\alpha)$ on $S_z$; the condition on $G$ means $(X-\alpha)G + 1$ vanishes on all $k+\sigma$ points of $S_z$, but this polynomial has degree $\le k$, which is impossible since $k+\sigma > k$.

Since no such $G$ can exist, noncontainment is automatic for any witness with $|S_z| = k+\sigma \ge k+1$—the rational function $-1/(X-\alpha)$ is genuinely rational and can't be explained by a low-degree polynomial on any large set. So the count reduces to a purely combinatorial problem.

I need to count the distinct slopes $z = Q_S(\alpha)$ arising from all supports $S$ where $w|_S$ is interpolable by a degree-$\le k$ polynomial. This is equivalent to finding codewords in $\RS[\F,D,k+1]$ that agree with $w$ on at least $k+\sigma$ points, then evaluating each at $\alpha$ and counting distinct values. The number of such distinct slopes is bounded by the list-decoding size of this Reed-Solomon code at agreement threshold $k+\sigma$.

So the $t=1$ arbitrary-anchor residue cloud reduces exactly to the locator-fiber list problem at dimension $k+1$ — meaning the $t=1$ case is fundamentally as hard as the arbitrary-word list-decoding conjecture, not easier. The reduction shows this isn't something we can prove unconditionally without resolving the open conjecture. The projection $P \mapsto P(\alpha)$ can only help by collapsing values, but doesn't fundamentally change the difficulty. The question of whether the reverse implication holds — whether a polynomial bound on the anchored cloud would force the locator-fiber list to be polynomial — remains unclear because of that projection.

Now the adversary's strategy becomes clearer: they pick $\alpha$ to maximize the spread of distinct values $\{P(\alpha)\}$ across the list of degree-$\le k$ polynomials. If the list itself is polynomial-sized, the cloud stays polynomial regardless of $\alpha$. But if the list is exponential (which can occur below corrected reserve or for quotient-periodic $w$), we need to determine whether the projection actually collapses it down. The aperiodic case is where the real content lies.

So the $t=1$ arbitrary-anchor bound turns out to be equivalent to the arbitrary-word locator list conjecture at dimension $k+1$ — there's no independent elementary proof here, and the projection only helps the upper bound. The inhomogeneous norm sieve doesn't "close" at $t=1$ as hoped; the $t=1$ case already contains the full arbitrary-word list-decoding problem. Let me verify this is right though. The anchored datum uses a completely arbitrary word $w$, and the supports that contribute are those where $w$ agrees with a degree-$\le k$ polynomial on $k + \sigma$ points — that's exactly the dimension-$(k+1)$ list for arbitrary $w$. So as $w$ ranges over all words, we're looking at arbitrary-word lists for $\RS[F,D,k+1]$, and the support set is precisely an arbitrary-word locator fiber.

Now I need to verify the reduction respects the prefix structure. The locator fiber $\Fib_U(s)$ counts supports where the degree of $U \bmod L_S$ is less than $k+1$, and by the fiber proposition, the number of distinct codewords appearing as $Q_S$ for valid $S$ equals the list size at dimension $k+1$ for $w$ agreeing on at least $k+\sigma$ points. The number of distinct slopes at $\alpha$ is bounded by the number of distinct codewords, which is bounded by the fiber size.

So the exact formula holds: the number of distinct slopes equals the list size of dimension-$(k+1)$ codewords agreeing with $w$ on $\delta = 1 - (k+\sigma)/n$ fraction of points. The catch is that this list can be superpolynomial precisely when $w$ is quotient-core at dimension $k+1$, which matches the structure of the final locator conjecture — those are the charged exceptions. For aperiodic $w$, the conjecture predicts polynomial lists, but it remains open. The $t=1$ theorem essentially reduces to solving the list conjecture at dimension $k+1$. The real question is whether the projection to slope values $P(\alpha)$ could be polynomial even when the full list isn't — whether there's a quotient-periodic-free word where the list explodes but the distinct slopes stay manageable.

Two codewords $P_1, P_2$ give the same slope exactly when $P_1(\alpha) = P_2(\alpha)$, which happens iff $\alpha$ is a root of their difference (degree $\le k$, so at most $k$ roots). The fibers of the projection are sets of codewords sharing a common value at $\alpha$, and the number of distinct slopes is the list size divided by the average fiber size. The adversary would choose $\alpha$ to minimize collisions.

For a random $\alpha \in \F \setminus D$, any two distinct codewords collide in at most $k$ values of $\alpha$, so the expected number of colliding pairs is at most $\binom{L}{2} \cdot k / (q - n)$. If $q$ is polynomial and $L$ is polynomial, this becomes manageable.

The real tension emerges when $L$ is exponential. If $q$ is large enough relative to $L^2 k$, the adversary could potentially construct an exponential slope cloud, but that would simultaneously refute the list conjecture—so no new counterexample to RCUT beyond what the conjecture already rules out. Conversely, if $q$ is small (polynomial), then any list collapses under projection to at most $q$ distinct values in $\F$, making the slope cloud trivially bounded by $q$. The MCA error becomes $\Lambda / q$, and we're aiming for $\Lambda \le n^{O(1)}$ rather than just $\Lambda \le q$.

In the polynomial-field regime, this bound is automatic since $\Lambda \le q$ is already polynomial. The hard regime is when $q$ grows exponentially, say $2^{\omega(\log n)}$ up to $2^{o(n)}$. The nontrivial regime for the bound $\Lambda \le n^{O(1)}$ is precisely where $q \gg n^{O(1)}$.

The projection argument doesn't obviously help when the aperiodic list $L$ is unknown—if $L$ is conjecturally polynomial, projection preserves that, but without knowing it, we can't rely on projection to save us in large $q$. So the cleaner approach is to recognize that the $t=1$, $E=X-\alpha$, $B=1$ anchored residue cloud is exactly the image of the dimension-$(k+1)$ arbitrary-word list under evaluation-at-$\alpha$, which gives us noncontainment automatically and reduces the slope cloud size to counting distinct values.

This means the $t=1$ RCUT bound for aperiodic $w$ is actually implied by the dimension-$(k+1)$ locator conjecture—it's not independent, and the secant/norm-sieve route can't bypass it because it contains the full arbitrary-word list problem. The Cycle94 plan's hope for an inhomogeneous norm sieve fails because $w$ is arbitrary, so there's no characteristic-zero moment structure to exploit; the monomial norm-sieve relied on special structure like $U_c = X^s + \ldots$, which an arbitrary anchor destroys. But this is actually good news: the $t=1$ MCA wall and the list wall are the same wall, so they can be attacked together.

Now I'm checking whether there's any unconditional partial result we can extract. In the quasi-polynomial split-prime range $p > \exp(O((\log n)^2))$, the list conjecture is known for monomial-prefix words, but the arbitrary-word version remains fully open. Let me reconsider whether the $t=1$ anchored problem might be provable via the secant lemma combined with projection. From L-CYCLE94 at $t=1$, distinct bad slopes have supports intersecting in at most $k$ points, giving us a constant-weight code with minimum distance $\ge 2\sigma$. By the Plotkin/GV bound this can be exponentially large, so the secant bound alone yields nothing—that route is indeed cut off. But looking at the projection to slopes themselves, I need to count distinct slopes rather than supports. So the bad slopes are exactly the image of the list $\mathcal{L}$ under evaluation at $\alpha$, meaning $\Lambda \le |\mathcal{L}|$. The key insight is that whether the exact $t=1$ theorem holds reduces to whether this eval-at-$\alpha$ image stays polynomial, which is precisely what conj:final-locator asks. I can state this as a conditional result plus extract some partial unconditional facts: noncontainment is automatic and always holds, and the slope mechanism fundamentally comes down to this evaluation map.

For the quasi-polynomial regime, the bottleneck is clear — I'd need to extend the Galois no-collision argument beyond monomial-prefix words to arbitrary words. The current no-collision theorem works for prefix fibers where two supports $S, S'$ collide if their codewords $P_S, P_{S'}$ are equal, but the arbitrary-word case has a different structure where I'm checking whether the finite-field list is actually larger than the characteristic-zero list.

The paper leaves this open as a conjecture, and the obstruction is that arbitrary words lack the clean character-zero structure. But I'm wondering if the anchored evaluation at $t=1$ — where we mod out by $E = X - \alpha$ and evaluate $P(\alpha)$ — might still give us something to work with. Let me try a different angle using the Johnson bound: if every pair of list elements agrees on at most $k$ points with each other, but each agrees with $w$ on at least $k + \sigma$ points, then in the Johnson regime where agreement exceeds roughly $\sqrt{(k+1)n}$, I should get polynomial list size. The issue is that the deployed regime sits below the Johnson threshold, which is precisely why this approach needs refinement.

Actually, let me recalculate the Johnson parameters more carefully. The Johnson radius is $\delta_J = 1 - \sqrt{\rho'}$ where $\rho' \approx \rho = (k+1)/n$. Our actual decoding radius is $\delta = 1 - (k+\sigma)/n = 1 - \rho - \eta$ where $\eta = \sigma/n$. For us to be above Johnson, I need $1 - \rho - \eta < 1 - \sqrt{\rho}$, which means $\rho + \eta > \sqrt{\rho}$. Since $\rho < \sqrt{\rho}$ for $\rho < 1$, this holds exactly when $\eta > \sqrt{\rho}(1 - \sqrt{\rho})$, which turns out to be a constant threshold.

But here's the issue: $\eta = \sigma/n$ with $\sigma \geq Cn/\log n$, so $\eta = \Theta(1/\log n) \to 0$. This means $\eta$ is much smaller than the constant $\sqrt{\rho}(1 - \sqrt{\rho})$, so actually $\rho + \eta < \sqrt{\rho}$, which means our radius $1 - \rho - \eta > 1 - \sqrt{\rho}$. We're operating beyond the Johnson radius in the hard regime where list-decoding bounds don't constrain us, confirming this is genuinely hard and connects to the open list conjecture.

Now I can formulate the answer as a clean reduction theorem: for the setup with $(E,B,w) = (X-\alpha, 1, w)$ where $\alpha \notin D$, I can establish that any witness with agreement $|S| \geq k+1$ is noncontained, and the bad-slope set corresponds exactly to the list-decoding solutions.

The key insight is that the $t=1$ anchored problem is equivalent to the arbitrary-word list problem at dimension $k+1$ and radius $1-\rho-\eta$. But here's where the secant/norm-sieve route fails: it can't prove the $t=1$ bound because that bound already encodes the full arbitrary-word list problem, which lacks the characteristic-zero moment structure needed for prime-averaging or monomial norm sieves.

I need to verify whether this equivalence truly holds both directions or if I only have the one-way reduction from $t=1$ anchored to list-decoding. I need to be precise about which direction of the equivalence matters for the upper bound. The cloud-to-list implication is what I actually need: if the cloud polynomial is bounded, that constrains the list size. The reverse direction gets murky in finite fields, especially when the field isn't huge, but for the prize-relevant regime I should just stick with the clean direction. 

Now looking at the actual field parameters: the normal form has $\emca = \frac{1}{q}\max_t \Lambda^{NC}_t$ where all the field sizes align, and I'm targeting $\Lambda^{NC} \le n^{1+o(1)}$. At $t=1$, the cloud size is bounded by the list size, so if the locator conjecture holds with list size $n^B$, then $\Lambda^{NC}_1 \le n^B$. The generated field size $q_{\text{gen}}$ affects both the entropy reserve and the dimension shift in the $t=1$ reduction—when I move from $t=0$ to $t=1$, the rate increases by $1/n$ and the number of challenges decreases by one, but the entropy constraint stays essentially stable.

The key question is whether a counterexample could exist: that would require an aperiodic superpolynomial list whose evaluation at $\alpha$ remains superpolynomial, which would directly contradict the locator conjecture. Since quotient periodicity is the only known source of superpolynomial behavior and it's explicitly excluded, there's no mechanism for such a counterexample. So the $t=1$ statement is true conditional on the locator conjecture holding at dimension $k+1$, and the reduction is exact.

Now I'm checking whether there's an elementary proof I might have missed by examining the evaluation map more carefully. If two codewords agree at $\alpha$ and also agree on overlapping sets of size at most $k$, then their difference is a polynomial of degree at most $k$ with at least $k+1$ roots (including $\alpha$ and the intersection points), which forces it to be zero.

This means codewords sharing the same value at $\alpha$ must have disjoint agreement sets, which is a useful structural constraint. I can reformulate this by writing any codeword through $(\alpha, z)$ as $P(X) = z + (X - \alpha)R(X)$ where $R$ has degree at most $k-1$, and the agreement condition becomes a constraint on $R$ matching a transformed word. So the codewords with a fixed slope $z$ correspond exactly to the list-decoding problem for this transformed word, and the total count of distinct slopes bounds the number of codewords. The core obstruction is that for arbitrary words, the coefficients in the difference of interpolants aren't bounded by $\{-1,0,1\}$ anymore, so the archimedean norm argument that worked for monomials breaks down—there's no way to control the size of lifted elements in $\Z[\zeta]$ without additional structure on the word itself. The real issue is that the coefficient bound breaks down when $w$ is arbitrary—I need to either replace the $\|h\|_1 \le 2s$ constraint with something that handles arbitrary anchors, or restrict to a fixed prime. Actually, the key insight is that what matters isn't the support list itself being small, but rather the image of the codewords $P_S$ at $\alpha$, so the collision theorem should target those directly rather than the supports. The cleanest formulation is just the arbitrary-word generalization of the no-collision result, which identifies the precise obstruction: loss of the $\{-1,0,1\}$ coefficient control. On whether the anchor can be removed—it can't, since $w$ is the entire content; setting $w = 0$ collapses the problem to explaining bad slopes for $-z/(X-\alpha)$ alone. The charged part of the quotient-periodic list follows the same bookkeeping as the conjecture, just shifted through the dimension change. For the aperiodic part, I'm targeting $n^{1+o(1)}$. Let me verify the field usage: the slope $z$ lives in $\F_q$ (line, code, and challenge field all align), giving $\emca = \Lambda/q$. The generator field $q_D$ enters through the reserve and entropy condition, scaled by $\rho + 1/n$ but asymptotically unchanged. The $2^{-128}$ is the challenge error target; to beat it with our upper bound $\emca \le n^{1+o(1)}/q$, we'd need $q > 2^{128} n^{1+o(1)}$.

Now I'm exploring whether there's a sharper unconditional partial result by combining the secant bound with a counting argument on distinct slopes. If I have distinct slopes $z_1, \ldots, z_\Lambda$ with corresponding codewords $P_1, \ldots, P_\Lambda$ (all distinct since the slopes are distinct), each agreeing with $w$ on a support of size $k+\sigma$, then the secant lemma gives pairwise agreement $|S_i \cap S_j| \le k$ for $i \ne j$. Since $P_i(\alpha) \ne P_j(\alpha)$, the polynomial $P_i - P_j$ doesn't vanish at $\alpha$, so I can't improve this bound further.

This means the family $\{S_i\}$ forms a constant-weight code with weight $k+\sigma$ and minimum distance $2\sigma$. By the Johnson bound, the number of such codewords can be exponentially large, so there's no unconditional polynomial bound on the number of slopes. The secant structure alone doesn't constrain things enough—this confirms the route cut. The fact that the polynomial doesn't vanish at $\alpha$ doesn't help with the counting argument either.

So the unconditional content boils down to: automatic noncontainment, the exact reduction to the dimension-$(k+1)$ list image, and the trivial bound $\le q$. The conditional content relies on RCUT with $t=1$ following from the final-locator conjecture. The route cut itself stems from the secant and norm-sieve approach being unable to improve beyond the arbitrary-word list problem. Now I'm checking this reduction against the toy script to verify it correctly enumerates supports of size $k+\sigma$, interpolates $w$ on each support to get a polynomial, and validates it.

The script computes the slope $z$ by taking the ratio of the polynomial modulo $E = X - \alpha$ versus $B$ modulo $E$. For $t=1$, this gives $z = Q_S(\alpha)$ where $Q_S$ is the degree-$\le k$ interpolant of $w$ on support $S$—exactly my reduction. The script groups supports by slope, which confirms the eval-at-$\alpha$ approach operationalizes the reduction correctly. Now I'm verifying once more that every witness is noncontained by checking against the residue definition: with $B=1$ and $E = X-\alpha$, noncontainment requires no polynomial $G$ of degree $<k$ satisfying $G(x) = -1/(x-\alpha)$ for all $x$ in the slope class $S_z$. Since $(X-\alpha)G(X) + 1$ would have degree $\le k$ and vanish on $S_z$ (which has size $k+\sigma$), this is impossible. The maximal support set $S_z$ can be larger than $k+\sigma$, but what matters is the codeword $Q_z$ itself—the slope $z = Q_z(\alpha)$ is determined by which degree-$\le k$ polynomial agrees with $w$ on at least $k+\sigma$ points. So the count of distinct slopes equals the count of distinct evaluations $Q_z(\alpha)$ across all such codewords. Now I'll write up the final answer, leading with the verdict and then the five required sections, followed by the self-audit addressing the six questions—I'll preserve all the theorem and lemma labels and use minimal formatting with display equations where needed.

The prompt's named atom is L-CYCLE95-ANCHORED-PERFIBER-COLLISION-T1, which I'll resolve by showing it equals the dimension-$(k+1)$ arbitrary-word locator list local limit. For the classification tag, I have a bankable reduction lemma (the cloud equals the evaluation list, with automatic noncontainment), a route cut (the secant/norm-sieve can't prove $t=1$ because it reduces to the full arbitrary-word list problem), and an exact new wall (the arbitrary-word no-collision generalization). Since the reduction theorem is the concrete deliverable, I'll lead with BANKABLE_LEMMA and note the others, though this ultimately reduces the wall to a known open conjecture. I'm verifying the dimension and degree bounds for the residue code—the witness polynomial has degree at most $k$, so the code $\RS[F,D,k+1]$ has dimension $k+1$ with agreement threshold $k+\sigma$, which translates to slack $\sigma-1$ relative to that dimension. The reserve condition holds unconditionally. Now I need to write out the exact unconditional source-valid replacement theorem, starting with the $t=1$ anchored reduction statement. For general $B \ne 1$, the slope is just $P(\alpha)/B$—a rescaling that doesn't change the count. So I can assume $B = 1$ without loss of generality, and the bad-slope set equals exactly $\{P(\alpha) : P \in \mathcal{L}\}$, with the inequality accounting only for potential collisions under the map. This affine color normalization directly addresses one of the self-audit items, confirming the numerator is invariant under $B$-rescaling and $w$-translation. Now I'm ready to write the final answer. For the field usage parameters, all three query sizes normalize to $q$, which keeps the slopes within $\F_q$, while the generator parameter $q_D$ gets reserved and adjusted through the $\rho$ shift. The security bound of $2^{-128}$ translates directly to needing the list size bounded by $2^{-128}q$, which matches exactly what the final-locator conjecture requires over a large field.
